name: Future and Plans (ㄹ 거예요)
summary: Talk about the future and your intentions with the -(으)ㄹ 거예요 ending.
explanation:
The most common way to express the future in Korean is -(으)ㄹ 거예요. It covers both plans ('I will / I'm going to') and guesses ('it'll probably'). Attach -ㄹ 거예요 to a stem ending in a vowel and -을 거예요 to a stem ending in a consonant: 가다 becomes 갈 거예요 (galgeoyeyo, I'll go), 먹다 becomes 먹을 거예요 (meogeulgeoyeyo, I'll eat).

This ending is often shortened to 거예요 in writing and pronounced like 꺼예요. It works for nearly any verb and is the go-to future for daily conversation, from 'I'll call you' to 'It'll rain tomorrow'.

For stronger intention, especially a promise to the listener, Korean uses -(으)ㄹ게요: 제가 할게요 (jega halgeyo, I'll do it). The -ㄹ게요 form signals a commitment you make on the spot, while -ㄹ 거예요 states a plan or prediction more neutrally.
pattern: vowel stem + ㄹ 거예요 / consonant stem + 을 거예요

examples.native: 내일 친구를 만날 거예요. (naeil chingureul mannal geoyeyo.)
주말에 영화를 볼 거예요. (jumare yeonghwareul bol geoyeyo.)
examples.en: I'm going to watch a movie this weekend.

examples.native: 저녁에 한국어를 공부할 거예요. (jeonyeoge hangugeoreul gongbuhal geoyeyo.)
examples.en: I'll study Korean in the evening.

examples.native: 내일 비가 올 거예요. (naeil biga ol geoyeyo.)

examples.en: I'll go with you next time.

commonMistakes: Using 을 거예요 after a vowel stem. After a vowel use ㄹ 거예요: 갈 거예요, not 가을 거예요.
Confusing -ㄹ 거예요 (plan or guess) with -ㄹ게요 (promise to the listener).
Writing 거예요 as 거에요. The correct spelling is 거예요.
Forgetting the space before 거예요. It is two words: 갈 거예요.
Using -ㄹ게요 for a weather forecast. Predictions take -ㄹ 거예요, since you are not promising anything.
tip: Plans and predictions use -ㄹ/을 거예요. When you promise the listener something on the spot, switch to -ㄹ게요.
